title: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
This project builds a custom dataset by combining ETF options chains, price histories, top-fund holdings, earnings calendars, and AI-scored news sentiment for four ETFs spanning different investment styles: VOO, QQQ, ARKQ, and BOTZ.
Two research questions drive the analysis:

title: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
Deliverables



Documentation & Analysis



Reference
API docs for all 21 package functions


Tutorial
Install → load data → analyze → visualize


Technical Report
Methodology, findings, and limitations


Full Notebook
9-section Jupyter notebook with visualizations






Project Links



Streamlit App
Interactive dashboard (live)


GitHub Repository
Source code and data

title: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
The etf_iv Package
The etf_iv Python package exposes 21 functions across four categories. All collected data is bundled as CSV files so the full analysis runs offline — no network access or API keys required.
pip install -e .
from etf_iv import load_options_filtered, compute_iv_skew

options = load_options_filtered()   # loads bundled data instantly
skew    = compute_iv_skew(options)
print(skew[["atm_iv", "otm_iv", "iv_skew"]])

Function categories



Category
Count
Description




Bundled Loaders
6
Load pre-collected CSV data with no network access


Live Collection
5
Fetch fresh data from yfinance and Anthropic


Cleaning
6
Standardize, filter, and enrich raw DataFrames


Analysis
4
Compute PCR, IV skew, forward returns, earnings proximity

title: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
Data Sources

yfinance — 6-month OHLCV price history and full options chains
stockanalysis.com — Top-25 holdings and next earnings dates (scraped with robots.txt compliance)
Anthropic Claude API — AI-scored news sentiment on a 1–5 scale

title: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
Key Findings

Broad-market ETFs (VOO, QQQ) carry structurally high put/call ratios from institutional hedging, making raw PCR a poor standalone directional signal.
IV skew is persistently positive across all four ETFs, consistent with sustained demand for crash protection.
ETFs whose top holdings have nearer earnings tend to show steeper IV skews, offering directional support for the “earnings fear” hypothesis.


Built with Python, yfinance, Streamlit, and the Anthropic Claude API.

title: Technical Report: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
STAT 386 Final Project — Jillian Baker



Options markets encode forward-looking information that price history alone cannot capture. When traders buy puts aggressively, they are paying for downside protection — and that collective behavior shows up in two measurable signals: the put/call ratio (PCR) and implied volatility skew. This project asks whether those signals contain meaningful information about future returns and earnings risk for four ETFs that span different investment styles:



ETF
Focus




VOO
S&P 500 broad market


QQQ
Nasdaq-100 large-cap tech


ARKQ
ARK autonomous & robotics (actively managed)


BOTZ
Global X robotics & AI (index-based)



Two research questions guided the analysis:

Do extreme put/call ratios predict short-term forward returns?
Does implied-volatility skew correlate with earnings proximity of top holdings?





All data was collected in a reproducible, compliance-conscious pipeline:
Price history — Six months of daily OHLCV data for all four ETFs via the yfinance library. Data was cleaned for duplicates, missing values, and dtype consistency.
Options chains — Full listed strikes across every expiration date, fetched via yfinance. Each contract was joined with the underlying spot price to compute moneyness (strike / spot). Contracts with fewer than 1 trade in volume or fewer than 10 in open interest were dropped as illiquid.
Holdings and earnings — Top-25 holdings and their portfolio weights were scraped from stockanalysis.com for each ETF. Next earnings dates were scraped for each individual holding ticker. Scraping compliance was verified via robots.txt before any requests were made, and a 1.5-second delay was enforced between requests.
News sentiment — Recent headlines for each ETF were retrieved via yfinance and scored on a 1–5 scale (1 = very bearish, 5 = very bullish) using the Anthropic Claude API with structured tool-use output for consistency.
All collected data is bundled as CSV files inside the etf_iv package so the full analysis can be reproduced without any network access or API keys.






The put/call ratio was computed two ways for each ETF:

Aggregate PCR — total put volume divided by total call volume across all expirations, as well as the same ratio using open interest instead of volume.
Expiration-bucketed PCR — a separate PCR computed for each individual expiration date, bucketed into near (≤30 days), mid (31–90 days), and far (>90 days) horizons. This increases the number of observations beyond the four-ticker cross-section.

Threshold-based signals were applied: PCR > 1.5 was labeled bearish and PCR < 0.5 was labeled bullish. Forward returns of 1 day and 5 days were computed from price history using percentage change with a forward shift. Pearson and Spearman correlations were then computed between PCR and forward returns.



IV skew was defined as the difference between median OTM put implied volatility and median ATM put implied volatility:

IV Skew = median(OTM Put IV) − median(ATM Put IV)

ATM puts were defined as moneyness in [0.97, 1.03] and OTM puts as moneyness in [0.85, 0.97). The analysis was restricted to options with 15–60 days to expiration to capture the earnings-window premium while avoiding ultra-short-dated noise.
Earnings proximity was measured as a weight-normalized days-to-earnings metric: for each ETF’s top-10 holdings, the days until each holding reports earnings were averaged using the holding’s portfolio weight as the weight. This gives heavier-weighted constituents more influence on the proximity score. An OLS trend line was fit to test whether ETFs with nearer earnings exhibit steeper skews.







Broad-market ETFs (VOO, QQQ) carry structurally higher put/call ratios driven by institutional hedging activity. This means that a raw PCR reading of 1.5 or higher for these funds reflects normal hedging behavior rather than genuine bearish sentiment — making PCR a poor standalone directional signal for large index ETFs.
Thematic ETFs (ARKQ, BOTZ) have smaller, less liquid options markets. Their PCR readings are more volatile and potentially more informative on a relative basis, but are also more susceptible to distortion from a small number of large trades.
Pearson and Spearman correlations between PCR and forward returns were directionally suggestive but lacked statistical power given only four cross-sectional observations. The expiration-bucketed approach increased observations but could not establish causality.



IV skew was persistently positive across all four ETFs, meaning OTM puts were consistently priced above ATM puts. This is consistent with well-documented demand for crash protection in equity options markets.
The cross-sectional comparison of IV skew against weighted days-to-earnings provided directional support for the “earnings fear” hypothesis — ETFs whose top holdings report sooner tend to show steeper skews. However, with only four data points this relationship cannot be established statistically. A stronger test would require daily IV snapshots tracked across the earnings window for each holding.






Single snapshot design — yfinance provides a current options chain, not a historical time series of IV or PCR. This means we cannot observe how these signals evolved before past price moves.
Small cross-sectional n — With only four ETFs, all correlations are illustrative rather than statistically conclusive.
Holdings staleness — ETF holdings are scraped at a single point in time and may not reflect intraday rebalancing.
Confounding factors — ETFs differ in sector composition, average market cap, liquidity, and recent volatility, all of which affect options pricing independently of earnings proximity or sentiment.





All data is bundled with the package. To reproduce the full analysis:
git clone https://github.com/jilliangbaker97/Final-Project-386.git
cd Final-Project-386
python -m venv venv && source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt && pip install -e .
jupyter notebook final_project.ipynb
See the Tutorial for full setup instructions and the API Documentation for function references.





Streamlit App
GitHub Repository
Tutorial
API Documentation

Options markets encode forward-looking information that price history alone cannot capture. When traders buy puts aggressively, they are paying for downside protection — and that collective behavior shows up in two measurable signals: the put/call ratio (PCR) and implied volatility skew. This project asks whether those signals contain meaningful information about future returns and earnings risk for four ETFs that span different investment styles:



ETF
Focus




VOO
S&P 500 broad market


QQQ
Nasdaq-100 large-cap tech


ARKQ
ARK autonomous & robotics (actively managed)


BOTZ
Global X robotics & AI (index-based)



Two research questions guided the analysis:

Do extreme put/call ratios predict short-term forward returns?
Does implied-volatility skew correlate with earnings proximity of top holdings?

title: Technical Report: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
All data was collected in a reproducible, compliance-conscious pipeline:
Price history — Six months of daily OHLCV data for all four ETFs via the yfinance library. Data was cleaned for duplicates, missing values, and dtype consistency.
Options chains — Full listed strikes across every expiration date, fetched via yfinance. Each contract was joined with the underlying spot price to compute moneyness (strike / spot). Contracts with fewer than 1 trade in volume or fewer than 10 in open interest were dropped as illiquid.
Holdings and earnings — Top-25 holdings and their portfolio weights were scraped from stockanalysis.com for each ETF. Next earnings dates were scraped for each individual holding ticker. Scraping compliance was verified via robots.txt before any requests were made, and a 1.5-second delay was enforced between requests.
News sentiment — Recent headlines for each ETF were retrieved via yfinance and scored on a 1–5 scale (1 = very bearish, 5 = very bullish) using the Anthropic Claude API with structured tool-use output for consistency.
All collected data is bundled as CSV files inside the etf_iv package so the full analysis can be reproduced without any network access or API keys.

title: Technical Report: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
The put/call ratio was computed two ways for each ETF:

Aggregate PCR — total put volume divided by total call volume across all expirations, as well as the same ratio using open interest instead of volume.

Threshold-based signals were applied: PCR > 1.5 was labeled bearish and PCR < 0.5 was labeled bullish. Forward returns of 1 day and 5 days were computed from price history using percentage change with a forward shift. Pearson and Spearman correlations were then computed between PCR and forward returns.

IV Skew = median(OTM Put IV) − median(ATM Put IV)

Earnings proximity was measured as a weight-normalized days-to-earnings metric: for each ETF’s top-10 holdings, the days until each holding reports earnings were averaged using the holding’s portfolio weight as the weight. This gives heavier-weighted constituents more influence on the proximity score. An OLS trend line was fit to test whether ETFs with nearer earnings exhibit steeper skews.

title: Technical Report: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
Broad-market ETFs (VOO, QQQ) carry structurally higher put/call ratios driven by institutional hedging activity. This means that a raw PCR reading of 1.5 or higher for these funds reflects normal hedging behavior rather than genuine bearish sentiment — making PCR a poor standalone directional signal for large index ETFs.
Thematic ETFs (ARKQ, BOTZ) have smaller, less liquid options markets. Their PCR readings are more volatile and potentially more informative on a relative basis, but are also more susceptible to distortion from a small number of large trades.
Pearson and Spearman correlations between PCR and forward returns were directionally suggestive but lacked statistical power given only four cross-sectional observations. The expiration-bucketed approach increased observations but could not establish causality.



IV skew was persistently positive across all four ETFs, meaning OTM puts were consistently priced above ATM puts. This is consistent with well-documented demand for crash protection in equity options markets.
The cross-sectional comparison of IV skew against weighted days-to-earnings provided directional support for the “earnings fear” hypothesis — ETFs whose top holdings report sooner tend to show steeper skews. However, with only four data points this relationship cannot be established statistically. A stronger test would require daily IV snapshots tracked across the earnings window for each holding.

title: Technical Report: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
Single snapshot design — yfinance provides a current options chain, not a historical time series of IV or PCR. This means we cannot observe how these signals evolved before past price moves.
Small cross-sectional n — With only four ETFs, all correlations are illustrative rather than statistically conclusive.
Holdings staleness — ETF holdings are scraped at a single point in time and may not reflect intraday rebalancing.
Confounding factors — ETFs differ in sector composition, average market cap, liquidity, and recent volatility, all of which affect options pricing independently of earnings proximity or sentiment.

title: Technical Report: ETF Implied Volatility, Options Sentiment & Earnings Analysis
text:
Streamlit App
GitHub Repository
Tutorial
API Documentation

title: Live Collection
text: The five collection functions download live data from external sources. All require network access. The two sentiment functions additionally require the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable.

title: Live Collection
text:
get_price_history()

Download OHLCV price history for a single ticker via yfinance.

Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




ticker
str
required
Ticker symbol (e.g. "QQQ", "qqq")


period
str
"1y"
yfinance period string: "1d", "5d", "1mo", "3mo", "6mo", "1y", "2y", "5y", "10y", "ytd", "max"



Returns
pd.DataFrame with a DatetimeIndex and columns:



Column
Type
Description




Open
float
Opening price


High
float
Daily high


Low
float
Daily low


Close
float
Closing price


Volume
int
Trading volume


Dividends
float
Dividend amount


Stock Splits
float
Split factor


ticker
str
Ticker symbol (uppercase)



Raises
ValueError — if the ticker is invalid, the period string is unrecognized, or yfinance returns an empty response.
Example
from etf_iv import get_price_history

prices = get_price_history("QQQ", period="6mo")
print(prices.shape)
print(prices[["Open", "High", "Low", "Close", "Volume"]].tail(3))






Tip



After collecting, pass the result through clean_prices() before analysis to ensure consistent column names, dtypes, and sorting.

title: Live Collection
text:
get_options_chain()

Fetch the full options chain for every available expiration via yfinance.

Combines calls and puts from all listed expirations into a single DataFrame, adding expiration, option_type, and ticker columns.
Parameters



Parameter
Type
Default
Description




ticker
str
required
Ticker symbol (e.g. "AAPL", "VOO")



Returns
pd.DataFrame — all yfinance options fields plus:



Column
Type
Description




expiration
str
Expiration date (YYYY-MM-DD)


option_type
str
"call" or "put"


ticker
str
Ticker symbol (uppercase)



Raises
ValueError — if the ticker is invalid, has no listed options, or a network failure occurs.
Example
from etf_iv import get_options_chain

chain = get_options_chain("QQQ")
print(chain.shape)
print(chain[["expiration", "option_type", "strike", "impliedVolatility"]].head())

# Count options by expiration
print(chain.groupby(["expiration", "option_type"]).size())

title: Live Collection
text:
get_etf_holdings()

Fetch top ETF holdings and portfolio weights from stockanalysis.com.

Scrapes the holdings table from https://stockanalysis.com/etf/{ticker}/holdings/. Complies with robots.txt and enforces a 1.5-second request delay.
Parameters



Parameter
Type
Default
Description




ticker
str
required
ETF symbol (e.g. "SPY", "QQQ")



Returns
pd.DataFrame with columns:



Column
Type
Description




ticker
str
ETF symbol


holding_symbol
str
Holding’s ticker symbol


holding_name
str
Holding’s company name


weight_pct
float
Portfolio weight (0–100)



Raises
ValueError — if the ticker is invalid, the page structure has changed, or no holdings table is found.
Example
from etf_iv import get_etf_holdings

holdings = get_etf_holdings("QQQ")
print(holdings.nlargest(5, "weight_pct"))
#   ticker holding_symbol     holding_name  weight_pct
#      QQQ           MSFT  Microsoft Corp        8.92
#      QQQ           AAPL       Apple Inc        8.43
#   ...

title: Live Collection
text:
get_etf_headlines()

Fetch recent news headlines for an ETF ticker via yfinance.

Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




ticker
str
required
ETF symbol


max_articles
int
10
Maximum number of headlines to return



Returns
list[str] — headline strings, possibly empty if no recent news is available.
Example
from etf_iv import get_etf_headlines

headlines = get_etf_headlines("ARKQ", max_articles=10)
print(f"Fetched {len(headlines)} headlines")
for h in headlines[:3]:
    print(f"  • {h}")

title: Live Collection
text:
get_sentiment_score()

Score a list of news headlines 1–5 using Anthropic Claude’s tool-use API.

Uses Claude’s structured tool-use output (via the record_sentiment tool schema) to produce a consistent integer score. Falls back to 3 (Neutral) if no headlines are provided or if the API returns no tool-use block.






Warning



This function requires the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable:
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."   # macOS/Linux
set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...        # Windows


Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




ticker
str
required
ETF symbol (used as context in the prompt)


headlines
list[str]
required
Headline strings to score


model
str
"claude-sonnet-4-6"
Anthropic model ID (keyword-only)


client
anthropic.Anthropic \| None
None
Pre-built client; creates one from env var if None (keyword-only)



Returns
int — score from 1 to 5:



Score
Label




1
Very Bearish


2
Bearish


3
Neutral


4
Positive


5
Very Bullish



Example
from etf_iv import get_etf_headlines, get_sentiment_score

headlines = get_etf_headlines("ARKQ", max_articles=10)
score     = get_sentiment_score("ARKQ", headlines)
print(f"ARKQ sentiment: {score}/5")
# ARKQ sentiment: 4/5

# Pass a pre-built client to reuse across multiple calls
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
scores = {
    ticker: get_sentiment_score(ticker, get_etf_headlines(ticker), client=client)
    for ticker in ["VOO", "QQQ", "ARKQ", "BOTZ"]
}
print(scores)

title: Bundled Loaders
text: The six loader functions read CSV files bundled inside the etf_iv/data/ package directory. They work completely offline and require no API keys. All functions return a pd.DataFrame.

title: Bundled Loaders
text:
load_price_history()

Load bundled 6-month daily OHLCV price history for VOO, QQQ, ARKQ, and BOTZ.

Parameters
None.
Returns
pd.DataFrame with columns:



Column
Type
Description




ticker
str
ETF symbol (VOO, QQQ, ARKQ, BOTZ)


Date
datetime
Trading date (tz-naive)


Open
float
Opening price


High
float
Daily high price


Low
float
Daily low price


Close
float
Closing price


Volume
int
Trading volume



Example
from etf_iv import load_price_history

prices = load_price_history()
print(prices.shape)       # (504, 7)  — 4 tickers × ~126 trading days
print(prices.dtypes)
print(prices.head())
#   ticker       Date    Open    High     Low   Close    Volume
# 0   ARKQ 2024-10-01  ...

title: Bundled Loaders
text:
load_options_chain()

Load the full bundled options chain across all listed expirations (cleaned).

Parameters
None.
Returns
pd.DataFrame with columns:



Column
Type
Description




ticker
str
ETF symbol


expiration
str
Expiration date (YYYY-MM-DD)


contractType
str
"call" or "put"


strike
float
Strike price


bid
float
Bid price


ask
float
Ask price


volume
float
Contracts traded today


openInterest
float
Open interest


impliedVolatility
float
Implied volatility (annualized)









Tip



For most analysis use load_options_filtered(), which removes illiquid contracts and adds spot_price, moneyness, and dte.


Example
from etf_iv import load_options_chain

chain = load_options_chain()
print(chain.shape)
print(chain["contractType"].value_counts())
# call    ...
# put     ...

title: Bundled Loaders
text:
load_options_filtered()

Load the liquidity-filtered options chain with spot price, moneyness, and DTE.

Contracts are filtered to volume >= 1 and openInterest >= 10.
Parameters
None.
Returns
pd.DataFrame — all columns from load_options_chain() plus:



Column
Type
Description




spot_price
float
Underlying close price on collection date


moneyness
float
Strike / spot (1.0 = at-the-money)


dte
int
Calendar days to expiration from collection date



Example
from etf_iv import load_options_filtered

options = load_options_filtered()
print(options.shape)    # smaller than full chain — illiquid contracts removed

# Inspect moneyness distribution
print(options["moneyness"].describe())
# count    ...
# mean     ~1.0 (centered at ATM)
# ...

# Filter to near-the-money puts
atm_puts = options[
    (options["contractType"] == "put") &
    (options["moneyness"].between(0.95, 1.05))
]

title: Bundled Loaders
text:
load_etf_holdings()

Load bundled top-25 holdings and portfolio weights for each ETF, scraped from stockanalysis.com.

Parameters
None.
Returns
pd.DataFrame with columns:



Column
Type
Description




holding_ticker
str
Holding’s stock symbol


company_name
str
Holding’s company name


weight_pct
float
Portfolio weight (0–100)


etf_ticker
str
Parent ETF symbol



Example
from etf_iv import load_etf_holdings

holdings = load_etf_holdings()

# Top 5 holdings in QQQ by weight
qqq = holdings[holdings["etf_ticker"] == "QQQ"].nlargest(5, "weight_pct")
print(qqq[["holding_ticker", "company_name", "weight_pct"]])
#   holding_ticker         company_name  weight_pct
#   MSFT            Microsoft Corp         8.92
#   AAPL            Apple Inc              8.43
#   ...

title: Bundled Loaders
text:
load_earnings_dates()

Load bundled next-earnings dates for the top holdings of each ETF.

Parameters
None.
Returns
pd.DataFrame with columns:








Column
Type
Description




holding_ticker
str
Stock symbol of the holding


etf_ticker
str
Parent ETF symbol


earnings_date
datetime
Next scheduled earnings date (NaT if unavailable)



Example
from etf_iv import load_earnings_dates
import pandas as pd

earnings = load_earnings_dates()

# Holdings with earnings within the next 30 days
today = pd.Timestamp.today()
soon  = earnings[earnings["earnings_date"] <= today + pd.Timedelta(days=30)]
print(soon.sort_values("earnings_date"))

title: Bundled Loaders
text:
load_etf_sentiment()

Load bundled Anthropic Claude news-sentiment scores for each ETF.

Scores were computed with get_sentiment_score() over the top 10 recent headlines per ETF. The 1–5 scale maps to: 1 = Very Bearish, 2 = Bearish, 3 = Neutral, 4 = Positive, 5 = Very Bullish.
Parameters
None.
Returns
pd.DataFrame with columns:



Column
Type
Description




etf_ticker
str
ETF symbol


num_articles
int
Number of headlines scored


sentiment_score
int
1–5 composite score


sentiment_label
str
Human-readable label for the score



Example
from etf_iv import load_etf_sentiment

sentiment = load_etf_sentiment()
print(sentiment)
#   etf_ticker  num_articles  sentiment_score sentiment_label
# 0        VOO            10                4        Positive
# 1        QQQ            10                4        Positive
# 2       ARKQ            10                3         Neutral
# 3       BOTZ            10                3         Neutral

title: Analysis
text: The four analysis functions derive metrics from cleaned DataFrames. They perform no data fetching or cleaning — pass DataFrames from the Bundled Loaders or the Cleaning module.

title: Analysis
text:
compute_put_call_ratios()

Compute put/call ratios aggregated by ticker and by ticker-expiration.

Returns two DataFrames: one at the ticker level and one broken out by expiration. The expiration-level DataFrame also classifies each expiration into a horizon bucket (near, mid, far).
Parameters








Parameter
Type
Description




options
pd.DataFrame
Cleaned options with ticker, expiration, contractType, volume, openInterest



Returns
A tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame]:
pcr_ticker — indexed by ticker:



Column
Type
Description




pcr_volume
float
Put volume / Call volume


pcr_oi
float
Put open interest / Call open interest


put_volume
float
Total put volume


call_volume
float
Total call volume


put_oi
float
Total put open interest


call_oi
float
Total call open interest



pcr_expiry — one row per (ticker, expiration):








Column
Type
Description




ticker
str
ETF symbol


expiration
str
Expiration date


pcr_volume
float
Put/call volume ratio


pcr_oi
float
Put/call OI ratio


days_to_exp
int
Days from today to expiration


horizon
str
"near" (≤30d), "mid" (31–90d), or "far" (>90d)



Example
from etf_iv import load_options_filtered, compute_put_call_ratios

options = load_options_filtered()
pcr_ticker, pcr_expiry = compute_put_call_ratios(options)

print(pcr_ticker[["pcr_volume", "pcr_oi"]])
#         pcr_volume    pcr_oi
# ticker
# ARKQ      0.823456  0.912345
# BOTZ      1.123456  1.234567
# QQQ       1.456789  1.567890
# VOO       1.789012  1.890123

# Near-term expirations only
near = pcr_expiry[pcr_expiry["horizon"] == "near"]
print(near[["ticker", "expiration", "pcr_volume"]].head())






Tip



PCR > 1.5 is often used as a bearish signal (excess put buying relative to calls). PCR < 0.5 is treated as a bullish signal. However, broad-market ETFs like VOO and QQQ carry structurally elevated PCR from institutional hedging, making raw thresholds less reliable for those instruments.

title: Analysis
text:
compute_forward_returns()

Add N-day forward return columns to a price-history DataFrame.

For each trading-day row, computes the percentage change in Close N days forward. The last N rows for each ticker will have NaN forward returns (no future prices available).
Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




prices
pd.DataFrame
required
Cleaned prices with ticker, Date, Close


windows
list[int] \| None
[1, 5]
List of trading-day windows to compute



Returns
pd.DataFrame — copy of prices with one added column per window: fwd_1d, fwd_5d, etc. (percentage change, e.g. 0.012 = +1.2%).
Example
from etf_iv import load_price_history, compute_forward_returns

prices  = load_price_history()
returns = compute_forward_returns(prices, windows=[1, 5, 10])

print(returns[["ticker", "Date", "Close", "fwd_1d", "fwd_5d", "fwd_10d"]].tail(10))

# Check correlation between VOO 1-day returns and itself (sanity check)
voo = returns[returns["ticker"] == "VOO"].dropna(subset=["fwd_1d"])
print(voo[["fwd_1d", "fwd_5d"]].corr())

title: Analysis
text:
compute_iv_skew()

Compute IV skew as median OTM put IV minus median ATM put IV for each ETF.

Filters to put options within the specified DTE window, then computes the median implied volatility for ATM and OTM bands separately. The difference (otm_iv - atm_iv) is the skew — positive values mean OTM puts are priced higher than ATM puts (the typical “crash protection” premium).
Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




options
pd.DataFrame
required
Cleaned options with ticker, contractType, impliedVolatility, moneyness, dte


dte_range
tuple[int, int]
(15, 60)
(min_dte, max_dte) — inclusive DTE window for puts


atm_range
tuple[float, float]
(0.97, 1.03)
(lo, hi) moneyness band for ATM puts


otm_range
tuple[float, float]
(0.85, 0.97)
(lo, hi) moneyness band for OTM puts



Returns
pd.DataFrame indexed by ticker:



Column
Type
Description




atm_iv
float
Median implied volatility of ATM puts


otm_iv
float
Median implied volatility of OTM puts


iv_skew
float
otm_iv - atm_iv (positive = typical skew)


n_atm
int
Number of ATM put contracts in the DTE window


n_otm
int
Number of OTM put contracts in the DTE window



Example
from etf_iv import load_options_filtered, compute_iv_skew

options = load_options_filtered()
skew    = compute_iv_skew(options)

print(skew)
#         atm_iv    otm_iv  iv_skew  n_atm  n_otm
# ticker
# ARKQ    0.2345    0.3456   0.1111     12     28
# BOTZ    0.1987    0.2876   0.0889      8     19
# QQQ     0.1654    0.2345   0.0691     45    112
# VOO     0.1432    0.2123   0.0691     38     97

# Wider OTM band
deep_skew = compute_iv_skew(options, otm_range=(0.75, 0.95))
print(deep_skew[["atm_iv", "otm_iv", "iv_skew"]])






Note



A persistently positive iv_skew (OTM puts priced above ATM puts) is well-documented and reflects the market’s demand for downside crash protection. Thematic ETFs (ARKQ, BOTZ) tend to show steeper skew due to higher idiosyncratic risk and lower liquidity in their options markets.

title: Analysis
text:
compute_earnings_proximity()

Compute a weight-normalized “days until earnings” metric for each ETF.

For each ETF, takes the top-N holdings by weight, looks up their next earnings dates, and computes a weighted average of days-to-earnings. Also reports the fraction of holdings reporting within 30 and 60 days.
Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




holdings
pd.DataFrame
required
Cleaned holdings with holding_ticker, etf_ticker, weight_pct


earnings
pd.DataFrame
required
Cleaned earnings with holding_ticker, etf_ticker, earnings_date


today
pd.Timestamp \| None
None
Reference date; defaults to pd.Timestamp.today()


top_n
int
10
Number of largest holdings (by weight) per ETF to include



Returns
pd.DataFrame indexed by ticker (ETF symbol):








Column
Type
Description




weighted_dte_earnings
float
Weight-normalized days until next earnings


pct_within_30d
float
Fraction of top-N holdings reporting within 30 days


pct_within_60d
float
Fraction of top-N holdings reporting within 60 days


n_holdings_with_dates
int
Holdings with a valid earnings date



Example
from etf_iv import (
    load_etf_holdings, load_earnings_dates,
    compute_earnings_proximity,
)
import pandas as pd

holdings = load_etf_holdings()
earnings = load_earnings_dates()

proximity = compute_earnings_proximity(holdings, earnings, top_n=10)
print(proximity.sort_values("weighted_dte_earnings"))
#         weighted_dte_earnings  pct_within_30d  pct_within_60d  n_holdings_with_dates
# ticker
# ARKQ                     23.5            0.40            0.80                      9
# BOTZ                     31.2            0.30            0.60                      8
# QQQ                      45.8            0.20            0.50                     10
# VOO                      52.1            0.15            0.40                     10

# Use a fixed reference date for reproducible results
proximity_fixed = compute_earnings_proximity(
    holdings, earnings, today=pd.Timestamp("2025-04-01"), top_n=10
)






Tip



Combine this with compute_iv_skew() to test the “earnings fear” hypothesis: ETFs whose top holdings have nearer upcoming earnings should, in theory, show steeper IV skew as the market prices in event risk.

title: API Reference
text: The etf_iv package exposes 21 functions organized into four modules. Every function accepts and returns pd.DataFrame objects and raises ValueError on invalid input.

title: API Reference
text:
Bundled Loaders
Load pre-collected CSV data bundled with the package. Works completely offline — no network access or API keys required.
Full reference →



Function
Returns
Description




load_price_history()
DataFrame
6-month OHLCV for VOO, QQQ, ARKQ, BOTZ


load_options_chain()
DataFrame
Full options chain (all expirations, cleaned)


load_options_filtered()
DataFrame
Liquidity-filtered options with spot and moneyness


load_etf_holdings()
DataFrame
Top-25 holdings per ETF


load_earnings_dates()
DataFrame
Next earnings dates for ETF holdings


load_etf_sentiment()
DataFrame
Claude-scored news sentiment (1–5 scale)

title: API Reference
text:
Live Collection
Fetch fresh data from yfinance, stockanalysis.com, or the Anthropic API. Requires network access; sentiment functions additionally require ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.
Full reference →



Function
Returns
Description




get_price_history(ticker, period)
DataFrame
Download OHLCV for a single ticker


get_options_chain(ticker)
DataFrame
Fetch all listed options across every expiration


get_etf_holdings(ticker)
DataFrame
Scrape top holdings and weights


get_etf_headlines(ticker, max_articles)
list[str]
Fetch recent news headlines via yfinance


get_sentiment_score(ticker, headlines)
int
Score headlines 1–5 with Claude tool use

title: API Reference
text:
Cleaning
Standardize, filter, and enrich raw DataFrames before analysis. All functions return a cleaned copy without mutating the input.
Full reference →



Function
Returns
Description




clean_prices(df)
DataFrame
Standardize raw price-history DataFrame


clean_options(df)
DataFrame
Standardize raw options chain


filter_liquidity(df, min_volume, min_open_interest)
DataFrame
Remove illiquid option contracts


add_spot_and_moneyness(options, prices)
DataFrame
Merge spot price; compute moneyness and DTE


clean_holdings(df)
DataFrame
Normalize holdings column names and types


clean_earnings(df)
DataFrame
Parse and filter earnings dates

title: API Reference
text:
Analysis
Compute derived metrics from cleaned DataFrames. No raw fetching or cleaning — pass cleaned DataFrames from the loaders or cleaning module.
Full reference →



Function
Returns
Description




compute_put_call_ratios(options)
tuple[DataFrame, DataFrame]
PCR by ticker and by ticker-expiration


compute_forward_returns(prices, windows)
DataFrame
Add N-day forward return columns


compute_iv_skew(options, dte_range, atm_range, otm_range)
DataFrame
IV skew = OTM put IV − ATM put IV


compute_earnings_proximity(holdings, earnings, today, top_n)
DataFrame
Weight-normalized days until earnings

title: Cleaning
text: The six cleaning functions transform raw collected DataFrames into analysis-ready form. Every function returns a cleaned copy — the input DataFrame is never mutated.

title: Cleaning
text:
clean_prices()

Standardize a raw price-history DataFrame from get_price_history() or a CSV.

Parameters








Parameter
Type
Description




df
pd.DataFrame
Raw price DataFrame; must contain ticker, Close, and either a Date column or a DatetimeIndex



Returns
pd.DataFrame with columns ticker, Date, Open, High, Low, Close, Volume — consistent dtypes, tz-naive dates, sorted by [ticker, Date].
Raises
ValueError — if df is empty or missing essential columns (ticker, Close).
Example
from etf_iv import get_price_history, clean_prices

raw    = get_price_history("QQQ", period="6mo")
prices = clean_prices(raw)

print(prices.dtypes)
# ticker            object
# Date      datetime64[ns]
# Open             float64
# High             float64
# Low              float64
# Close            float64
# Volume             int64

print(prices.head())

title: Cleaning
text:
clean_options()

Standardize a raw options chain from get_options_chain().

Renames option_type to contractType if present, enforces canonical column names and dtypes, drops rows missing strike or impliedVolatility, and sorts by [ticker, expiration, contractType, strike].
Parameters



Parameter
Type
Description




df
pd.DataFrame
Raw options chain from get_options_chain()



Returns
pd.DataFrame with canonical columns: ticker, expiration, contractType, strike, bid, ask, volume, openInterest, impliedVolatility, plus any additional yfinance fields.
Raises
ValueError — if df is empty or missing required columns.
Example
from etf_iv import get_options_chain, clean_options

raw     = get_options_chain("VOO")
options = clean_options(raw)

print(options[["ticker", "expiration", "contractType", "strike", "impliedVolatility"]].head())
print(f"Rows before cleaning: {len(raw)}, after: {len(options)}")

title: Cleaning
text:
filter_liquidity()

Remove illiquid option contracts that fall below volume or open-interest thresholds.

Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




df
pd.DataFrame
required
Cleaned options DataFrame with volume and openInterest columns


min_volume
int
1
Minimum daily volume (inclusive)


min_open_interest
int
10
Minimum open interest (inclusive)



Returns
pd.DataFrame — filtered copy with reset index.
Example
from etf_iv import get_options_chain, clean_options, filter_liquidity

chain    = clean_options(get_options_chain("QQQ"))
filtered = filter_liquidity(chain, min_volume=1, min_open_interest=10)

print(f"Contracts before: {len(chain)}, after filtering: {len(filtered)}")
print(filtered["volume"].min())   # >= 1
print(filtered["openInterest"].min())  # >= 10

title: Cleaning
text:
add_spot_and_moneyness()

Merge the most recent spot price into an options DataFrame and compute moneyness and DTE.

For each option contract, the function looks up the latest Close for its ticker from prices, attaches it as spot_price, then computes:

moneyness = strike / spot_price (1.0 = at-the-money, <1 = OTM call/ITM put, >1 = ITM call/OTM put)
dte = calendar days from today to expiration

Parameters








Parameter
Type
Description




options
pd.DataFrame
Cleaned options with ticker, strike, expiration


prices
pd.DataFrame
Cleaned prices with ticker, Date, Close



Returns
pd.DataFrame — options with three added columns:



Column
Type
Description




spot_price
float
Latest closing price for the ticker


moneyness
float
strike / spot_price


dte
int
Calendar days from today to expiration



Raises
ValueError — if any ticker in options has no matching price rows in prices.
Example
from etf_iv import (
    get_price_history, get_options_chain,
    clean_prices, clean_options, filter_liquidity, add_spot_and_moneyness,
)

prices  = clean_prices(get_price_history("QQQ", period="6mo"))
options = filter_liquidity(clean_options(get_options_chain("QQQ")))
enriched = add_spot_and_moneyness(options, prices)

print(enriched[["strike", "spot_price", "moneyness", "dte"]].head())
#    strike  spot_price  moneyness  dte
#     400.0      480.5   0.8325    12
#     420.0      480.5   0.8741    12
#     ...

# Near-the-money puts expiring in 15–60 days
atm_puts = enriched[
    (enriched["contractType"] == "put") &
    (enriched["moneyness"].between(0.97, 1.03)) &
    (enriched["dte"].between(15, 60))
]

title: Cleaning
text:
clean_holdings()

Normalize holdings column names and types, handling both package and notebook naming conventions.

Accepts either holding_symbol/ticker (from get_etf_holdings()) or holding_ticker/etf_ticker naming (from CSV), and normalizes to a consistent schema.
Parameters








Parameter
Type
Description




df
pd.DataFrame
Raw holdings DataFrame from get_etf_holdings() or a CSV



Returns
pd.DataFrame with normalized columns:



Column
Type
Description




holding_ticker
str
Holding’s stock symbol


etf_ticker
str
Parent ETF symbol


holding_name
str
Company name (if present)


weight_pct
float
Portfolio weight (0–100)



Raises
ValueError — if df is empty.
Example
from etf_iv import get_etf_holdings, clean_holdings

raw      = get_etf_holdings("BOTZ")
holdings = clean_holdings(raw)

print(holdings.dtypes)
# holding_ticker     object
# etf_ticker         object
# holding_name       object
# weight_pct        float64

title: Cleaning
text:
clean_earnings()

Parse earnings date strings and filter out exchange-prefixed tickers.

Removes tickers with exchange prefixes (containing : or /) that represent non-US-listed securities. Parses earnings_date strings to datetime; unparseable values become NaT (kept in output).
Parameters








Parameter
Type
Description




df
pd.DataFrame
Raw earnings DataFrame; must contain holding_ticker and earnings_date



Returns
pd.DataFrame — exchange-prefixed tickers removed; earnings_date cast to datetime64[ns] (with NaT for unparseable values).
Raises
ValueError — if df is empty.
Example
from etf_iv import clean_earnings
import pandas as pd

raw = pd.DataFrame({
    "holding_ticker": ["AAPL", "XLON:BP", "MSFT", "TSX:RY"],
    "etf_ticker":     ["QQQ",   "BOTZ",  "QQQ",   "BOTZ"],
    "earnings_date":  ["2025-01-28", None, "2025-01-29", "2025-02-15"],
})

cleaned = clean_earnings(raw)
print(cleaned)
#   holding_ticker etf_ticker earnings_date
# 0           AAPL        QQQ    2025-01-28
# 1           MSFT        QQQ    2025-01-29
# (Exchange-prefixed rows removed)

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text: This tutorial walks you through installing the etf_iv package and using it to explore ETF options data, holdings, and news sentiment — with and without network access.

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
Prerequisites

Python 3.11 or later
Git

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
1. Clone the Repository
git clone https://github.com/jilliangbaker97/Final-Project-386.git
cd Final-Project-386

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
section: 2. Create a Virtual Environment and Install
text:
2. Create a Virtual Environment and Install
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate        # Windows: venv\Scripts\activate

pip install -r requirements.txt
pip install -e .                 # installs the etf_iv package in editable mode






Note



The -e . install makes the etf_iv package importable from anywhere in your environment. It also includes the bundled CSV datasets in etf_iv/data/.

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
section: 3. Load the Bundled Dataset (No API Keys Needed)
text:
3. Load the Bundled Dataset (No API Keys Needed)
All data is pre-collected and bundled with the package. Every loader returns a pd.DataFrame and works completely offline.
from etf_iv import (
    load_price_history,
    load_options_chain,
    load_options_filtered,
    load_etf_holdings,
    load_earnings_dates,
    load_etf_sentiment,
)

# 6-month daily OHLCV for VOO, QQQ, ARKQ, BOTZ
prices = load_price_history()
print(prices.head())
#    ticker       Date    Open    High     Low   Close     Volume
# 0    ARKQ 2024-10-01  ...

# Full options chain (all expirations, calls and puts)
options_chain = load_options_chain()
print(f"Options chain shape: {options_chain.shape}")

# Liquidity-filtered options with spot price and moneyness
options = load_options_filtered()
print(f"Filtered options shape: {options.shape}")

# Top-25 holdings per ETF
holdings = load_etf_holdings()
print(holdings[holdings["etf_ticker"] == "QQQ"].head(3))

# Earnings dates for each holding
earnings = load_earnings_dates()
print(earnings.head())

# Claude-scored sentiment (1 = very bearish, 5 = very bullish)
sentiment = load_etf_sentiment()
print(sentiment)
#   etf_ticker  num_articles  sentiment_score sentiment_label
# 0        VOO            10                4        Positive






Tip



Use load_options_filtered() rather than load_options_chain() for analysis. The filtered dataset removes illiquid contracts (volume < 1, open interest < 10) and adds spot_price, moneyness, and dte columns.

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
4. Run the Analysis Functions

Put/Call Ratios
from etf_iv import load_options_filtered, compute_put_call_ratios

options = load_options_filtered()

# Returns two DataFrames: ticker-level and expiration-level PCR
pcr_ticker, pcr_expiry = compute_put_call_ratios(options)

print(pcr_ticker[["pcr_volume", "pcr_oi"]])
#         pcr_volume    pcr_oi
# ticker
# ARKQ      0.823456  0.912345
# BOTZ      1.123456  1.234567
# QQQ       1.456789  1.567890
# VOO       1.789012  1.890123

print(pcr_expiry[["ticker", "expiration", "pcr_volume", "horizon"]].head())


IV Skew
from etf_iv import compute_iv_skew

skew = compute_iv_skew(options)
print(skew[["atm_iv", "otm_iv", "iv_skew"]])
#         atm_iv    otm_iv  iv_skew
# ticker
# ARKQ    0.2345    0.3456   0.1111
# BOTZ    0.1987    0.2876   0.0889
# QQQ     0.1654    0.2345   0.0691
# VOO     0.1432    0.2123   0.0691


Forward Returns
from etf_iv import load_price_history, compute_forward_returns

prices  = load_price_history()
returns = compute_forward_returns(prices, windows=[1, 5])
print(returns[["ticker", "Date", "Close", "fwd_1d", "fwd_5d"]].tail())


Earnings Proximity
from etf_iv import load_etf_holdings, load_earnings_dates, compute_earnings_proximity

holdings = load_etf_holdings()
earnings = load_earnings_dates()

proximity = compute_earnings_proximity(holdings, earnings)
print(proximity[["weighted_dte_earnings", "pct_within_30d", "pct_within_60d"]])
#         weighted_dte_earnings  pct_within_30d  pct_within_60d
# ticker
# ARKQ                     23.5            0.40            0.80
# BOTZ                     31.2            0.30            0.60
# QQQ                      45.8            0.20            0.50
# VOO                      52.1            0.15            0.40

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
5. Visualize the Results

IV Skew Bar Chart
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from etf_iv import load_options_filtered, compute_iv_skew

options = load_options_filtered()
skew    = compute_iv_skew(options)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
x = range(len(skew))
ax.bar(x, skew["atm_iv"],  label="ATM IV",  alpha=0.7)
ax.bar(x, skew["iv_skew"], bottom=skew["atm_iv"], label="Skew (OTM − ATM)", alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(skew.index)
ax.set_ylabel("Implied Volatility")
ax.set_title("IV Skew by ETF")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


PCR Heatmap by Expiration
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
from etf_iv import load_options_filtered, compute_put_call_ratios

options = load_options_filtered()
_, pcr_expiry = compute_put_call_ratios(options)

pivot = pcr_expiry.pivot_table(
    index="ticker", columns="horizon", values="pcr_volume", aggfunc="mean"
)
sns.heatmap(pivot, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="RdYlGn_r")
plt.title("Average PCR by Ticker and Horizon")
plt.show()

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
6. Clean Raw Data (If Collecting Fresh)
If you collect fresh data with the live functions, use the cleaning module to standardize it before analysis.
from etf_iv import (
    get_price_history, get_options_chain,
    clean_prices, clean_options, filter_liquidity, add_spot_and_moneyness,
)

# Fetch raw data
raw_prices  = get_price_history("QQQ", period="6mo")
raw_options = get_options_chain("QQQ")

# Clean and enrich
clean_px   = clean_prices(raw_prices)
clean_opts = clean_options(raw_options)
filtered   = filter_liquidity(clean_opts, min_volume=1, min_open_interest=10)
enriched   = add_spot_and_moneyness(filtered, clean_px)

print(enriched[["ticker", "strike", "spot_price", "moneyness", "dte"]].head())

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
section: 7. Collect Fresh Sentiment Scores (Requires Anthropic API Key)
text:
7. Collect Fresh Sentiment Scores (Requires Anthropic API Key)






Warning



Sentiment scoring requires an Anthropic API key. Set the environment variable before running:
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."   # macOS/Linux
set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...        # Windows


from etf_iv import get_etf_headlines, get_sentiment_score

headlines = get_etf_headlines("ARKQ", max_articles=10)
print(headlines[:3])
# ['ARKQ hits 52-week high...', 'AI robotics ETF sees inflows...', ...]

score = get_sentiment_score("ARKQ", headlines)
print(f"ARKQ sentiment: {score}/5")
# ARKQ sentiment: 4/5
Scores are on a 1–5 scale:



Score
Label




1
Very Bearish


2
Bearish


3
Neutral


4
Positive


5
Very Bullish

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
8. Run the Streamlit Dashboard Locally
streamlit run streamlit_app.py
Open http://localhost:8501 in your browser. The app loads the bundled dataset automatically — no API keys or network access needed.
Or visit the live deployed app.

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
9. Run the Full Analysis Notebook
jupyter notebook final_project.ipynb
The notebook is organized into 9 sections. Each section loads bundled data by default, with optional “Collect Fresh Data” cells you can run separately.



Section
Content




1
Scraping compliance check


2
Price history collection & cleaning


3
Options chain collection & cleaning


4
Spot price join & moneyness


5
Liquidity filtering


6
ETF holdings scraping


7
Earnings dates scraping


8
News sentiment scoring (Claude API)


9
Put/Call ratio analysis & visualization


10
IV skew vs. earnings proximity analysis

title: Tutorial: ETF Implied Volatility Explorer
text:
Project Structure
etf_iv/                    # Installable Python package
  __init__.py              # Public API (21 functions)
  data_collection.py       # Options chains, price history, holdings scraping
  sentiment.py             # Claude-powered headline sentiment scoring
  cleaning.py              # Data cleaning and filtering
  analysis.py              # PCR, forward returns, IV skew, earnings proximity
  data/                    # Bundled CSV datasets
docs/                      # This Quarto documentation site
final_project.ipynb        # Main analysis notebook
streamlit_app.py           # Interactive Streamlit dashboard
requirements.txt           # Pinned dependency versions
pyproject.toml             # Package metadata
